English: to get; to obtain; to be able to

etymology: From Proto-Vietic *ʔəh (“to stay”); cognate with Muong ớ.  Most prepositions in Vietnamese were originally verbs: cho (“to give; for”), đến (“to come; to”), tới (“to arrive; to”), lên (“to go up; onto”), vào (“to enter; in, into”), theo (“to follow; according to”), về (“to return; about”).  Similarity to Chinese 於 is coincidental, as semantically the Vietnamese word is a grammaticalized verbal and phonetically there is a tonal mismatch. From Proto-Tai *ˀjuːᴮ (“to be (in a place)”). Cognate with Northern Thai ᩀᩪ᩵, Lao ຢູ່ (yū), Lü ᦊᦴᧈ (ẏuu1), Tai Dam ꪤꪴ꪿, Shan ယူႇ (yùu), Ahom 𑜊𑜥 (jʸū), Bouyei qyus, Zhuang youq.

From Proto-Tai *ɢwɯnᴬ (“human being; person”). Cognate with Northern Thai ᨤᩫ᩠ᨶ, Lao ຄົນ (khon), Lü ᦅᦳᧃ (kun), Shan ၵူၼ်း (kúun), Tai Nüa ᥐᥨᥢᥰ (kön), Ahom 𑜀𑜤𑜃𑜫 (kun), Bouyei wenz, Zhuang vunz, Zuojiang Zhuang koenz. Nôm form of con (“child”).

Outside Tai-Kadai: compare Proto-Mon-Khmer *tiʔ ~ tiiʔ ~ tiəʔ (“that yonder”), Mon တေံ (teʔ, “that”), Khmer ទី (tii), Vietnamese thì (“then, which, to be”). Compare Chinese 地 (MC diɪH), Malay di.

Cognate with Northern Thai ᨩᩦ᩶, Lao ຊີ້ (sī), Lü ᦋᦲᧉ (tsii2), Tai Dam ꪋꪲ꫁, Shan ၸီႉ (tsḭ̂i), Ahom 𑜋𑜣 (chī), Nong Zhuang jix. Compare Vietnamese chỉ, chĩa.

Cognate with Northern Thai ᨶ᩶ᩣᩴ (nam), Southern Thai หน่าม, Lao ນ້ຳ (nam), Lü ᦓᧄᧉ (nam2), Tai Dam ꪙꪾ꫁ (nam6), Shan ၼမ်ႉ (nâ̰m), Tai Nüa ᥘᥛᥳ (lam5), Khamti ꩫမ်ႇ, Aiton ꩫံ (naṃ), Phake ꩫံ (naṃ), Ahom 𑜃𑜪 (naṃ), Bouyei ramx, Zhuang raemx, Nong Zhuang naemx, Zuojiang Zhuang naemx, Saek นัม. etymology: From Middle Vietnamese già, from Proto-Vietic *k-raː, from Proto-Mon-Khmer *kraʔ ~ *(k)rah. Cognate with Muong khà, Tho [Cuối Chăm] kʰraː¹, Bahnar kră. Compare also Nghệ An/Hà Tĩnh dialects tra.  The standard written Vietnamese form is irregular (the expected form would be **sa), while the North Central form shows the shift from *k-r to *k-l observable in several other instances: compare sâu and trâu (“caterpillar”), sừng and trừng (“horn”). See giòi (“maggot”) for another instance of ‹gi› from initial *k-r.

etymology: From Proto-Vietic *cih (“to point”); probably related to Sino-Vietnamese word from 指 (“finger”). From Proto-Tai *ɟiːᶜ (“to point”), from Middle Chinese 指 (MC t͡ɕˠiɪX, “finger; to point; to indicate”). Cognate with Northern Thai ᨩᩦ᩶, Lao ຊີ້ (sī), Lü ᦋᦲᧉ (tsii2), Tai Dam ꪋꪲ꫁, Shan ၸီႉ (tsḭ̂i), Ahom 𑜋𑜣 (chī), Nong Zhuang jix. Compare Vietnamese chỉ, chĩa.

etymology: From Proto-Vietic *cih (“to point”); probably related to Sino-Vietnamese word from 指 (“finger”). From Proto-Tai *ɟiːᶜ (“to point”), from Middle Chinese 指 (MC t͡ɕˠiɪX, “finger; to point; to indicate”). Cognate with Northern Thai ᨩᩦ᩶, Lao ຊີ້ (sī), Lü ᦋᦲᧉ (tsii2), Tai Dam ꪋꪲ꫁, Shan ၸီႉ (tsḭ̂i), Ahom 𑜋𑜣 (chī), Nong Zhuang jix. Compare Vietnamese chỉ, chĩa.
From Proto-Vietic *muːs (“nose”), from Proto-Mon-Khmer *muh ~ *muuh ~ *muus (“ nose”); cognates include Khmer ច្រមុះ (crɑmoh), Bahnar muh, Khasi khmut, Mon မိုဟ်, Car Nicobarese meh.
Non-Sino-Vietnamese reading of Chinese 爸 (“father”, SV: bả).
From Middle Vietnamese 𥚇 (lưởi), from Proto-Vietic *laːs. Cognate with Nghệ An /Hà Tĩnh dialects lại, Muong lãi. Compare also Pacoh leah (“to lick”).
From Middle Vietnamese 𦹵 (cỏ), from Proto-Vietic *kɔh (“grass”). Cognate with Thavung ก็อฮ.

Compare Proto-Katuic *ʔapaal (“shoulder”) (whence Pacoh apal). Thai บ่า  Indonesian Bahu  Some North Central dialects have the form ban with unlenited ‹b› (vs. standard form with lenited ‹v›) and ‹-n› reflex of earlier *-l.

From Middle Vietnamese 𥚇 (lưởi), from Proto-Vietic *laːs. Cognate with Nghệ An /Hà Tĩnh dialects lại, Muong lãi. Compare also Pacoh leah (“to lick”).
From Proto-Vietic *-naŋʔ.
Non-Sino-Vietnamese reading of Chinese 望 (“to expect”, SV: vọng). Compare Thai มอง (mɔɔng).

From Proto-Southwestern Tai *kʰwiːᴮ (“to ride”), from Middle Chinese 騎 (MC ɡˠiᴇ, “to ride”).[1] Cognate with Lao ຂີ່ (khī), Lü ᦃᦲᧈ (ẋii1), Tai Dam ꪄꪲ꪿, Shan ၶီႇ (khìi), Ahom 𑜁𑜣 (khī), Zhuang gwih, Nong Zhuang kiq.
From Proto-Vietic *goːt. Cognate with Chut [Sách] koːt⁸.
Short for ngáo đá (“be high/intoxicated after using     methamphetamine”).  2. (colloquial) acting stupidly
From Middle Chinese 炭 (MC tʰɑnH, “charcoal”). Cognate with Northern Thai ᨳ᩵ᩣ᩠ᨶ, Lao ຖ່ານ (thān), Shan ထၢၼ်ႇ (thàan).
From Middle Chinese 炭 (MC tʰɑnH, “charcoal”). Cognate with Northern Thai ᨳ᩵ᩣ᩠ᨶ, Lao ຖ່ານ (thān), Shan ထၢၼ်ႇ (thàan).
From Proto-Vietic *roːŋʔ. Cognate with Northern Thai ᩁ᩵ᩬᨦ, Lao ຮ່ອງ (hǭng), Khün ᩁ᩵ᩬᨦ, Shan ႁွင်ႈ (hōang), Ahom 𑜍𑜨𑜂𑜫 (roṅ).
Non-Sino-Vietnamese reading of Chinese 幔 (SV: mạn).  Cognate with Northern Thai ᨾ᩵ᩣ᩠ᨶ, Lao ມ່ານ (mān), Khün ᨾ᩵ᩣ᩠ᨶ, Lü ᦙᦱᧃᧈ (maan1), Shan မၢၼ်ႈ (māan).
From Proto-Mon-Khmer *luh (“hole”); cognates include Khmer ធ្លុះ (thluh), Bahnar bơlôh, Central Mnong lôh, and proto-Palaungic *luuh.

From Proto-Southwestern Tai *ʔaːŋᴮ¹ (“basin”), from Proto-Tai *ʔaːŋᴮ (“basin”), from Middle Chinese 盎 (MC ʔɑŋX, ʔɑŋH, “an earthen vessel with a big belly and a small mouth”). Cognate with Northern Thai ᩋ᩵ᩣ᩠ᨦ, Lao ອ່າງ (ʼāng), Tai Dam ꪮ꪿ꪱꪉ, Shan ဢၢင်ႇ (ʼàang), Tai Nüa ᥟᥣᥒᥱ (ʼǎang), Ahom 𑜒𑜂𑜫 (ʼaṅ).
From Proto-Mon-Khmer *wiŋ ~ *wiiŋ ~ *wiəŋ ~ *waiŋ ~ *wəŋ ~ *wuəŋ ~ *wuŋ ~ *wuuŋ (“to go round; to turn”); cognate with Khmer វង់ (vŭəng, “circle”). Related to vồng, quầng, vầng.
Non-Sino-Vietnamese reading of Chinese 兔 (SV: thố).
From Middle Vietnamese căóc, cóc.

From Proto-Vietic *ɓaːk (“to carry (on shoulder)”) (though initial is irregular). Cognate with Northern Thai ᨷᩯ᩠ᨠ, Lao ແບກ (bǣk), Tai Dam ꪵꪚꪀ, Shan မႅၵ်ႇ (màek) or ဝႅၵ်ႇ (wàek), Ahom 𑜉𑜢𑜀𑜫 (mik), Nong Zhuang mbeg.
Cognate with Lao ບີບ (bīp), Tai Dam ꪚꪲꪚ, Shan မိပ်ႇ (mìp) or ဝိပ်ႇ (wìp).
(archaic or dialectal, Central Vietnam) Alternative form of đường (“road”)       □ 1944, Lưu Hữu Phước (lyrics and music), “Lên đàng [Hit the Road]”:
From Proto-Vietic *r-kaː. Cognate with Thavung กา. Compare Khmer រកា (rɔkaa, “ Year of the Rooster”).
Non-Sino-Vietnamese reading of Chinese 膠 (“glue”, SV: giao), unattested. Cognate with Thai กาว (gaao), Lao ກາວ (kāo), Burmese ကော် (kau), Khmer កាវ (kaaw).

From Proto-Southwestern Tai *mɛːwᴬ² (“cat”), of onomatopoeic origin. Cognate with Northern Thai ᨾᩯ᩠ᩅ, Lao ແມວ (mǣu), Lü ᦶᦙᧁ (maew), Tai Dam ꪵꪣꪫ, Shan မႅဝ်း (máew), Tai Nüa ᥛᥦᥝᥴ (máew), Phake မိဝ် (miw), Ahom 𑜈𑜢𑜈𑜫 (biw). Compare Zhuang meuz, Chinese 貓, Vietnamese mèo, Khasi miaw.

From Proto-Tai *ˀjeːwᴬ (“waist”), from Middle Chinese 腰 (MC ʔiᴇu, “waist”). Cognate with Northern Thai ᩋᩯ᩠ᩅ, Lao ແອວ (ʼǣu), Tai Dam ꪵꪮꪫ, Shan ဢႅဝ် (ʼǎew), Zhuang yiu. Possibly related to เอี้ยว (îao, “to twist, to bend (one's body)”).
French 'bread' From Old Occitan, from Vulgar Latin *baneum, from Latin balneum.
From Proto-Mon-Khmer *ŋǝm ~ *ŋam (“to immerse, soak”); cognate with Muong ngâm, Khasi ngam (“to dive into”).
Sino-Vietnamese word from 勃 (“fine powder”), from an obsolete reading.
From Proto-Vietic *p-ɗuŋʔ ~ *p-ruŋʔ (“hot”).
Sino-Vietnamese word from 臆 (“chest”). Compare Proto-Tai *ʔɤkᴰ (“chest”), whence Thai อก (òk).
From Proto-Vietic *k-ləŋ (“back”). Compare Proto-Tai *ʰlaŋᴬ (“back”), whence Thai หลัง (lǎng).
From Proto-Tai *boːŋᴬ. Cognate with Lao ພອງ (phǭng) or ໂພງ (phōng), Shan ပွင်း (póang). Possibly related to โปง (bpoong), โป่ง (bpòong), โพรง (proong).

From Proto-Vietic *k-moːlʔ ~ *k-muːrʔ ~ *k-moːrʔ. Cognate with Proto-Katuic *kmuar (whence Pacoh camoar (“termite”)), Semai kemur (“caterpillar, maggot”), Khmer ខ្មើរ (khmaə, “flying ant”), ខ្មុល (khmol, “mole cricket”).
Cognate with Tho [Cuối Chăm] pʰrɛːk⁸.  Pacoh prec is likely a Vietic loan, due to the lack of cognates in other Katuic languages.
From Proto-Vietic *kaɲʔ.

From Middle Vietnamese 𤝞 (chuọt, “mouse”), from Proto-Vietic *k-ɟɔːt (“ squirrel”). Compare Chinese 鼠 and Japanese 鼠 (nezumi), both of which also refer to generic mouse or rat-like animals. Also compare Latin mūsculus (“ muscle”, literally “little mouse”) and Ancient Greek μῦς (mûs, “mouse; muscle”) .
